Diagnosis and Management of Retrograde Cricopharyngeal Dysfunction: A Systematic Review
  • Source: OTO Open, Vol. 8(4):e70014, 2024
  • Topic: Retrograde Cricopharyngeal Dysfunction (R-CPD) - diagnosis, treatment outcomes, and management
  • Author(s): Malhotra R, Khan H, Zaransky S, Celidonio J, Yan K, Kaye R
1. Overview of Retrograde Cricopharyngeal Dysfunction
  • R-CPD --> syndrome characterized by inability to belch, gurgling noises, excessive flatulence, abdominal bloating
  • First described by Bastian et al --> March 2019
  • Impaired relaxation/coordination of cricopharyngeus (CP) muscle during swallowing --> results in abelchia
  • Patient-driven awareness grew rapidly via Reddit (r/noburp - 30,000+ members) and TikTok before formal publication
  • No gold standard for diagnosis --> clinical diagnosis based on symptomatology + relief after BTX injection
  • Early identification/management --> significant improvement in patient quality of life
2. Study Methodology
  • Databases searched: PubMed, Scopus, EMBASE (inception to July 8, 2024)
  • Search terms: retrograde AND cricopharyngeal dysfunction / retrograde AND cricopharyngeus dysfunction / upper esophageal AND dysfunction AND inability AND to AND belch
  • PRISMA guidelines followed for systematic review
  • 17 studies ultimately met inclusion criteria after abstract/full-text screening
  • Bias assessment: AHRQ method evaluating selection, performance, attrition, detection, reporting bias
  • IRB exemption granted by institution
3. Patient Demographics and Characteristics
3.1 Population Overview
  • 637 total patients across 17 studies
  • 318 males (50%), 319 females (50%)
  • Mean age: 29 years [range 7–68]
  • Some patients --> lifelong symptoms; some parents reported difficulty burping as infants
3.2 Presenting Symptoms
  • Inability to belch (abelchia): 637 (100%)
  • Abdominal bloating: 530 (83%)
  • Gurgling noises: 478 (75%)
  • Excessive flatulence: 454 (71%)
  • Other symptoms (14%): hiccups, dysphagia, globus sensation, throat tightness, difficulty vomiting, excessive vomiting, chest pain, nausea
4. Diagnostic Evaluation
4.1 Overview of Workup
  • 219 (34%) patients underwent diagnostic evaluation before R-CPD diagnosis
  • Diagnostic tests did NOT explain symptom constellation
4.2 Imaging and Endoscopic Modalities
  • EGD (esophagogastroduodenoscopy): 103 patients (47%) --> most common
  • Swallow studies: 61 patients (28%)
  • CT scan: 14 patients (6%)
  • Colonoscopy: 2 patients (1%)
4.3 Manometry Findings
  • High-resolution impedance manometry (HRIM): 45 patients (21%)
  • Common manometric characteristics in R-CPD:
  • Absence of UES relaxation upon gastroesophageal reflux
  • Air entrapment --> reflected by high impedance levels
  • Secondary peristalsis to clear air from esophagus
  • Patients with refractory symptoms --> higher rates of ineffective swallowing on manometry
  • Refractory patients more likely to have diagnosis of:
  • Ineffective esophageal motility
  • Absent contractility
4.4 Laryngoscopy Findings
  • Performed in 22 patients (10%)
  • Abnormal incidental findings in 2 patients:
  • Vocal cord polyp
  • Incomplete glottic closure
4.5 Other Diagnostic Tests
  • Hydrogen breath test: 1 patient (<1%)
  • Gastric emptying scan: 1 patient (<1%)
4.6 Medical Therapy Trials (Failed)
  • Antireflux therapy: 99 patients (45%) --> no improvement
  • Antispasmodics: 2 patients (1%)
  • Anxiolytics: 1 patient (<1%)
4.7 Incidental Findings
  • EGD findings: gastritis or esophagitis in 14 patients
  • Swallow studies, CT, colonoscopy, hydrogen breath test, gastric emptying --> no abnormalities
5. Treatment Options
5.1 Botulinum Toxin (BTX) Injection
  • All 637 patients received BTX injection into CP muscle
  • Represented --> primary/initial treatment for R-CPD
5.1.1 Dosage Range
  • Initial dose range: 10–100 units
  • Most common initial dose: 50 units --> 204 patients (37.3%)
Dose (Units) Patients n (%)
10 U 1 (case report)
25–30 U multiple patients
50 U 204 (37.3%) - most common
60–75 U multiple patients
80 U multiple patients
100 U multiple patients
5.1.2 Injection Methods
  • Endoscopic (general anesthesia): 523 patients (79%) --> most common; CP muscle visualized
  • In-office awake transcutaneous EMG-guided: 113 patients (20%) --> direct or lateral approach
  • Catheter balloon technique: 1 case report; intranasal balloon inserted to identify CP muscle location under ultrasound guidance
5.1.3 BTX Subtype
  • 6 of 17 studies disclosed using botulinum toxin A
  • These represented: 342/637 patients (54%)
  • Remaining studies --> did not indicate subtype
5.2 Cricopharyngeal Myotomy
  • 6 patients ultimately received CP myotomy for recurrent symptoms
  • Indications: symptom recurrence after initial BTX injection(s)
  • Myotomy history in these 6 patients:
  • 3 patients had 1 prior BTX injection
  • 2 patients had 2 prior BTX injections
  • 1 patient had 4 prior BTX injections
5.3 Esophageal Balloon Dilatation
  • Not effective as standalone treatment
  • 1 patient --> dilatation before initial BTX --> no improvement
  • 2 patients --> dilatation alongside second BTX injection --> also failed long-term
5.4 Prior Unsuccessful Treatment
  • 1 patient received prior esophageal dilatation --> unsuccessful; later achieved relief with single BTX injection
6. Treatment Outcomes
6.1 Initial BTX Injection Outcomes
  • 554 patients (86.9%) --> improved symptoms after initial BTX injection
  • Follow-up periods varied up to 4 years
6.2 Refractory/Recurrent Symptoms
  • 53 patients had symptoms refractory to first BTX injection
  • Of these 53, initial dose reported in 25 patients:
  • 75 units: n=7 (28%)
  • 80 units: n=6 (24%)
  • 50 units: n=5 (20%)
  • 30 units: n=3 (12%)
  • 60 units: n=2 (8%)
  • 100 units: n=2 (8%)
6.3 Subsequent BTX Injections
  • All refractory patients received same or higher dose on subsequent injection(s)
  • 40 patients (76%) --> symptom resolution after 1 subsequent BTX injection
  • 2 patients --> resolution after 2 subsequent BTX injections
6.4 Cricopharyngeal Myotomy Outcomes
  • 4 of 6 patients (67%) --> long-term resolution without further intervention
  • Of the 2 patients WITHOUT improvement:
  • 1 patient --> lost to follow-up
  • 1 patient --> immediate relief but symptom recurrence over time
6.5 Overall Treatment Success
  • 44 patients (83%) --> eventual long-term symptom improvement with BTX or myotomy
  • Esophageal balloon dilatation: failed in all cases attempted alongside BTX
7. Complications and Side Effects
7.1 Overall Safety Profile
  • No serious or permanent complications reported across dosage range (10–100 units)
  • All postoperative complications --> transient
  • Complication rate did NOT vary by BTX dosage
7.2 Common Postoperative Complications
  • Transient dysphagia --> most common
  • Regurgitation
  • Increased reflux
  • Fever, mild swelling
  • Some patients --> required antibiotic intervention
7.3 Specific Study Complications
Complication Studies Reporting
Transient dysphagia Multiple studies
Throat pain Arnaert 2024
Reflux Siddiqui, Wajsberg
Transient shortness of breath Wajsberg 2021
Dysphonia Wajsberg 2021
Hoarseness Doruk 2023
Cellulitis (from pharyngeal tear) Hoesli 2020
Tooth pain Hoesli 2020
Laryngospasm Hoesli 2020
Pulmonary edema Hoesli 2020
7.4 Post-Myotomy Complications
  • No complications reported after myotomy
8. Key Clinical Findings and Patterns
8.1 Manometry as Prognostic Tool
  • Patients with refractory symptoms after initial BTX:
  • Higher rates of ineffective swallowing on manometry
  • More likely to have diagnosis of:
  • Ineffective esophageal motility
  • Absent contractility
  • May support use of UES manometry for risk stratification --> further research needed
8.2 Dose-Response Observations
  • Most common initial dose: 50 units (37.3%)
  • Refractory patients most commonly received:
  • 75 units (28%)
  • 80 units (24%)
  • Reasons for suboptimal response --> unclear; highlights need for future studies
8.3 Treatment Algorithm Pattern
  • Initial treatment: BTX injection (most effective at 50 units)
  • Refractory cases: Repeat BTX (same/higher dose) OR CP myotomy
  • CP myotomy success rate: 67% complete resolution regardless of prior BTX attempts
9. Diagnostic Utility Assessment
9.1 Established Diagnostic Tools in R-CPD
  • Fiberoptic Endoscopic Evaluation of Swallowing (FEES)
  • Esophageal manometry --> established efficacy for CPD workup
  • Modified barium swallow studies
9.2 Utility in R-CPD (Unknown/Variable)
  • Inconsistently utilized due to lack of dedicated research
  • Manometry findings supportive but predictive value unclear
9.3 Diagnostic Limitations
  • No gold standard --> clinical diagnosis based on:
  • Classic symptom constellation
  • Symptom relief after BTX injection (both diagnostic AND therapeutic)
10. Social Media and Awareness Impact
  • Reddit r/noburp community --> created 2014, grown to 30,000+ members
  • Patient-driven awareness predated formal publication (2019)
  • Social media platforms used for:
  • Information sharing
  • Support groups
  • Lists of specialists treating R-CPD
  • Patient testimonials
11. Limitations of Current Literature
  • Inconsistent follow-up periods --> potential bias in long-term efficacy assessment
  • Unable to calculate mean/median time to follow-up due to lack of individualized data
  • Average loss-to-follow-up time --> not reported by many studies
  • Refractory symptoms could NOT be stratified by dose due to reporting limitations
  • Mean age of 29 years may reflect --> younger demographic more active on social media (TikTok, Reddit)
12. Conclusions and Clinical Implications
  • R-CPD --> newly recognized syndrome (first described 2019)
  • Clinical recognition crucial for timely, appropriate management
  • BTX injection --> effective initial treatment with minimal transient side effects
  • Success rate: 86.9% after initial injection
  • Subsequent injections --> 80% resolution in refractory cases
  • CP myotomy or subsequent BTX injections --> reserved for refractory cases
  • Myotomy: 67% complete resolution even after multiple prior BTX injections
  • Esophageal manometry may aid diagnosis but requires further study for prognostic utility
  • Future research needed:
  • Optimal initial/subsequent BTX dosing
  • Clear indications for myotomy vs. repeat BTX
  • Long-term outcomes with standardized follow-up
